records.ety: [L. communicatus, p. p. of communicare to communicate, fr. communis common. See Commune, v. i.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Communicated; p. pr. & vb. n. Communicating.]
usage: -- To Communicate, Impart, Reveal. Communicate is the more general term, and denotes the allowing of others to partake or enjoy in common with ourselves. Impart is more specific. It is giving to others a part of what we had held as our own, or making them our partners; as, to impart our feelings; to impart of our property, etc. Hence there is something more intimate in imparting intelligence than in communicating it. To reveal is to disclose something hidden or concealed; as, to reveal a secret.

defs: To share in common; to participate in.
quotes: To thousands that communicate our loss.

defs: To impart; to bestow; to convey; as, to communicate a disease or a sensation; to communicate motion by means of a crank.
quotes: Where God is worshiped, there he communicates his blessings and holy influences.

defs: To make known; to recount; to give; to impart; as, to communicate information to any one.

quotes: He communicated those thoughts only with the Lord Digby.
notes: ☞ This verb was formerly followed by with before the person receiving, but now usually takes to after it.

defs: To share or participate; to possess or enjoy in common; to have sympathy.
quotes: Ye did communicate with my affliction.

defs: To give alms, sympathy, or aid.
quotes: To do good and to communicate forget not.

defs: To have intercourse or to be the means of intercourse; as, to communicate with another on business; to be connected; as, a communicating artery.
quotes: Subjects suffered to communicate and to have intercourse of traffic.
The whole body is nothing but a system of such canals, which all communicate with one another.

defs: To partake of the Lord's supper; to commune.
quotes: The primitive Christians communicated every day.
